title: Quick check: Parallel tools and structured output

questions.prompt: Your agent calls 4 independent search tools serially, taking 8 seconds per query. What's the right fix?

choices.text: Execute the tool_use blocks in parallel with Promise.all. Claude already returned them in a single response because the calls are independent; run them concurrently and total tool-execution time drops to the slowest tool, not the sum. Often a 4× speedup on multi-tool steps.

questions.explanation: Claude returns multiple tool_use blocks in one response when they're independent. The serial-vs-parallel decision is in YOUR runtime, not the model. Promise.all in your tool runner is the entire change — match results back by tool_use_id when constructing the next user message.

questions.prompt: You want strict structured output from Claude (typed fields, enums, nested arrays). What's the cleanest pattern?

choices.text: Free-form JSON in a text response, plus regex post-processing.

Define a tool whose input_schema matches your desired output. Force it with `tool_choice: { type: 'tool', name: '...' }`. Claude generates schema-valid arguments; you read them as typed data, no parsing. Use enums and required fields aggressively.

questions.explanation: Tool-call structured output is the cleanest Claude pattern for typed data. The schema is enforced, optional and required fields are first-class, and nested structures are reliable. Prefilling is great for short responses but tool calls beat it for anything with a real schema.
